Function: nnp_calc_energy_force Module: cp2k.psmp Source: nnp_force.F:62-281 [...] Coverage (incl. loops): 1.07% | (excl. loops): 0.00% 
/home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_force.F: 62 - 281
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


62:    SUBROUTINE nnp_calc_energy_force(nnp, calc_forces)


[...]


75:       TYPE(atomic_kind_type), DIMENSION(:), POINTER      :: atomic_kind_set


76:       TYPE(cp_logger_type), POINTER                      :: logger


77:       TYPE(cp_subsys_type), POINTER                      :: subsys


78:       TYPE(distribution_1d_type), POINTER                :: local_particles


79:       TYPE(particle_type), DIMENSION(:), POINTER         :: particle_set


80:       TYPE(section_vals_type), POINTER                   :: print_section


81:       TYPE(virial_type), POINTER                         :: virial


82: 


83:       CALL timeset(routineN, handle)


84: 


85:       NULLIFY (particle_set, logger, local_particles, subsys, &


86:                atomic_kind_set)


87:       logger => cp_get_default_logger()


88: 


89:       CPASSERT(ASSOCIATED(nnp))


90:       CALL nnp_env_get(nnp_env=nnp, particle_set=particle_set, &


91:                        subsys=subsys, local_particles=local_particles, &


92:                        atomic_kind_set=atomic_kind_set)


93: 


94:       CALL cp_subsys_get(subsys, &


95:                          virial=virial)


96: 


97:       calc_stress = virial%pv_availability .AND. (.NOT. virial%pv_numer)


98:       IF (calc_stress .AND. .NOT. calc_forces) CPABORT('Stress cannot be calculated without forces')


99: 


100:       ! Initialize energy and gradient:


101:       nnp%atomic_energy(:, :) = 0.0_dp


102:       IF (calc_forces) nnp%myforce(:, :, :) = 0.0_dp


103:       IF (calc_forces) nnp%committee_forces(:, :, :) = 0.0_dp


104:       IF (calc_stress) nnp%committee_stress(:, :, :) = 0.0_dp


105: 


106:       !fill coord array


107:       ig = 1


108:       DO i = 1, nnp%n_ele


109:          DO j = 1, nnp%num_atoms


110:             IF (nnp%ele(i) == particle_set(j)%atomic_kind%element_symbol) THEN


111:                DO m = 1, 3


112:                   nnp%coord(m, ig) = particle_set(j)%r(m)


113:                END DO


114:                nnp%atoms(ig) = nnp%ele(i)


115:                CALL get_ptable_info(nnp%atoms(ig), number=nnp%nuc_atoms(ig))


116:                nnp%ele_ind(ig) = i


117:                nnp%sort(ig) = j


118:                nnp%sort_inv(j) = ig


119:                ig = ig + 1


120:             END IF


121:          END DO


122:       END DO


123: 


124:       ! Parallelisation: compute each rank's atom slice analytically.


125:       ! mecalc(p) = base+1 for p < remainder, base otherwise.  istart is the prefix sum.


126:       ! No allgather needed — the formula is deterministic given num_atoms, num_pe, mepos.


127:       n_base = nnp%num_atoms/logger%para_env%num_pe


128:       n_remainder = MOD(nnp%num_atoms, logger%para_env%num_pe)


129:       IF (logger%para_env%mepos < n_remainder) THEN


130:          mecalc = n_base + 1


131:          istart = 1 + logger%para_env%mepos*(n_base + 1)


132:       ELSE


133:          mecalc = n_base


134:          istart = 1 + n_remainder*(n_base + 1) + (logger%para_env%mepos - n_remainder)*n_base


135:       END IF


136:       


137:       ! reset extrapolation status


138:       nnp%output_expol = .FALSE.


139: 


140:       ! Prepare cell-list cache once per force eval (Verlet-skin amortised rebuild).


141:       ! Subsequent nnp_compute_neighbors calls then walk only the 27 bins around each centre atom.


142:       CALL nnp_prepare_cell_list_cache(nnp)


[...]


150:       IF (calc_forces) THEN


151:          max_nsym = 0


152:          DO ie = 1, nnp%n_ele


153:             IF (nnp%arc(ie)%n_nodes(1) > max_nsym) max_nsym = nnp%arc(ie)%n_nodes(1)


154:          END DO


155:          ! Only allocate if it hasn't been allocated yet, or if the system grew


156:          IF (.NOT. ALLOCATED(dsymdxyz) .OR. SIZE(dsymdxyz, 3) /= nnp%num_atoms) THEN


157:             IF (ALLOCATED(dsymdxyz)) THEN


158:                DEALLOCATE (dsymdxyz, active_atoms, active_flag, denergydsym)


159:                IF (ALLOCATED(stress)) DEALLOCATE (stress)


160:             END IF


161:             ALLOCATE (dsymdxyz(max_nsym, 3, nnp%num_atoms), SOURCE=0.0_dp)


162:             ALLOCATE (active_atoms(nnp%num_atoms))


163:             ALLOCATE (active_flag(nnp%num_atoms), SOURCE=0)


164:             ALLOCATE (denergydsym(max_nsym))


165:          END IF


166:          IF (calc_stress .AND. .NOT. ALLOCATED(stress)) ALLOCATE (stress(3, 3, max_nsym))


167:       END IF


168: 


169:       ! calc atomic contribution to energy and force


170:       DO i = istart, istart + mecalc - 1


171: 


172:          ! determine index of atom type and offset


173:          ind = nnp%ele_ind(i)


174: 


175:          ! reset input nodes of ele(ind):


176:          nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(:) = 0.0_dp


177: 


178:          ! compute sym fnct values


179:          IF (calc_forces) THEN


180:             !reset input grads of ele(ind):


181:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node_grad(:) = 0.0_dp


182:             IF (calc_stress) THEN


183:                stress(:, :, 1:nnp%arc(ind)%n_nodes(1)) = 0.0_dp


184:                CALL nnp_calc_acsf(nnp, i, dsymdxyz, stress, &


185:                                   active_atoms=active_atoms, active_flag=active_flag, &


186:                                   n_active=n_active)


187:             ELSE


188:                CALL nnp_calc_acsf(nnp, i, dsymdxyz, &


189:                                   active_atoms=active_atoms, active_flag=active_flag, &


190:                                   n_active=n_active)


191:             END IF


192:          ELSE


193:             CALL nnp_calc_acsf(nnp, i)


194:          END IF


195: 


196:          DO i_com = 1, nnp%n_committee


197:             ! predict energy


198:             CALL nnp_predict(nnp%arc(ind), nnp, i_com)


199:             nnp%atomic_energy(i, i_com) = nnp%arc(ind)%layer(nnp%n_layer)%node(1) + &


200:                                           nnp%atom_energies(ind)


201: 


202:             ! predict forces — restrict the accumulation to atoms touched by this centre's


203:             ! neighbour walk (active_atoms).  Drops O(num_atoms) inner loop to O(n_active).


204:             IF (calc_forces) THEN


205:                denergydsym(1:nnp%arc(ind)%n_nodes(1)) = 0.0_dp


206:                CALL nnp_gradients(nnp%arc(ind), nnp, i_com, denergydsym)


207:                IF (calc_stress) THEN


208:                   DO j = 1, nnp%arc(ind)%n_nodes(1)


209:                      nnp%committee_stress(:, :, i_com) = nnp%committee_stress(:, :, i_com) - &


210:                                                          denergydsym(j)*stress(:, :, j)


211:                   END DO


212:                END IF


213:                DO idx = 1, n_active


214:                   k = active_atoms(idx)


215:                   DO m = 1, 3


216:                      nnp%myforce(m, k, i_com) = nnp%myforce(m, k, i_com) - &


217:                         DOT_PRODUCT(denergydsym(1:nnp%arc(ind)%n_nodes(1)), &


218:                                     dsymdxyz(1:nnp%arc(ind)%n_nodes(1), m, k))


219:                   END DO


220:                END DO


221:             END IF


222:          END DO


223: 


224:          ! Selectively zero the dsymdxyz slice we just used and clear active_flag, so the


225:          ! buffer is clean for the next centre atom.  Cost is O(n_active) per centre.


226:          IF (calc_forces) THEN


227:             DO idx = 1, n_active


228:                k = active_atoms(idx)


229:                dsymdxyz(1:nnp%arc(ind)%n_nodes(1), :, k) = 0.0_dp


230:                active_flag(k) = 0


231:             END DO


232:          END IF


233: 


234:       END DO ! loop over num_atoms


235: 


236: 


237:       ! calculate energy:


238:       CALL logger%para_env%sum(nnp%atomic_energy(:, :))


239:       nnp%committee_energy(:) = SUM(nnp%atomic_energy, 1)


240:       nnp%nnp_potential_energy = SUM(nnp%committee_energy)/REAL(nnp%n_committee, dp)


241: 


242:       IF (calc_forces) THEN


243:          ! bring myforce to force array


244:          DO j = 1, nnp%num_atoms


245:             DO k = 1, 3


246:                nnp%committee_forces(k, (nnp%sort(j)), :) = nnp%myforce(k, j, :)


247:             END DO


248:          END DO


249:          CALL logger%para_env%sum(nnp%committee_forces)


250:          nnp%nnp_forces(:, :) = SUM(nnp%committee_forces, DIM=3)/REAL(nnp%n_committee, dp)


251:          DO j = 1, nnp%num_atoms


252:             particle_set(j)%f(:) = nnp%nnp_forces(:, j)


253:          END DO


254:       END IF


255: 


256:       IF (calc_stress) THEN


257:          CALL logger%para_env%sum(nnp%committee_stress)


258:          virial%pv_virial = SUM(nnp%committee_stress, DIM=3)/REAL(nnp%n_committee, dp)


259:       END IF


260: 


261:       ! Bias the standard deviation of committee disagreement


262:       IF (nnp%bias) THEN


263:          CALL nnp_bias_sigma(nnp, calc_forces)


264:          nnp%nnp_potential_energy = nnp%nnp_potential_energy + nnp%bias_energy


265:          IF (calc_forces) THEN


266:             DO j = 1, nnp%num_atoms


267:                particle_set(j)%f(:) = particle_set(j)%f(:) + nnp%bias_forces(:, j)


268:             END DO


269:          END IF


270:          ! print properties if requested


271:          print_section => section_vals_get_subs_vals(nnp%nnp_input, "BIAS%PRINT")


272:          CALL nnp_print_bias(nnp, print_section)


273:       END IF


274: 


275:       ! print properties if requested


276:       print_section => section_vals_get_subs_vals(nnp%nnp_input, "PRINT")


277:       CALL nnp_print(nnp, print_section)


278: 


279:       CALL timestop(handle)


280: 


281:    END SUBROUTINE nnp_calc_energy_force

Source file and linesnnp_force.F:62-281

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 502.00 to 42.00 cycles (11.95x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this function


Vectorization


Your function is probably not vectorized.
Only 11% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).


Details


Store and arithmetical SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).


Workaround


Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


512-bits vectorization


On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.


Workaround


Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high


  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

Workaround


Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

Type of elements and instruction set


12 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).

The binary function is composed of 12 FP arithmetical operations:

The binary function is storing 2243 bytes (280 double precision FP elements).

Source file and linesnnp_force.F:62-281

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 502.00 to 42.00 cycles (11.95x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this function


Vectorization


Your function is probably not vectorized.
Only 11% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).


Details


Store and arithmetical SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).


Workaround


Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


512-bits vectorization


On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.


Workaround


Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high


  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

12 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).
